items.title: BREAKING: Credit card serious delinquencies rose +0.4 percentage points in Q1 2026, to 13.1%, the highest since Q4 2010. This is only below the 2010 p...
items.description_html:
BREAKING: Credit card serious delinquencies rose +0.4 percentage points in Q1 2026, to 13.1%, the highest since Q4 2010.

This is only below the 2010 peak of 13.7%, in the aftermath of the 2008 Financial Crisis.

Since Q3 2022, serious credit card delinquencies have surged +5.5 percentage points, even larger than the +3.9 point increase in 2007-2010.

Furthermore, student loan 90+ days delinquencies jumped +0.7 percentage points in Q1 2026, to 10.3%, the highest since Q1 2020.

Auto loan serious delinquencies increased +0.4 percentage points, to 5.6%, the highest on record.

US consumers are falling behind debt at a crisis pace.

items.title: Risk appetite in South Korea is exploding: Margin loans outstanding on Korean stocks are up to a record $24.3 billion. Since the start of 2025, margin...
items.description_html:
Risk appetite in South Korea is exploding:

Margin loans outstanding on Korean stocks are up to a record $24.3 billion.

Since the start of 2025, margin debt has surged +140% and is up +32% since the beginning of this year alone.

To put this into perspective, the value of leveraged bets on Korean stocks was ~$5.0 billion in 2020.

This also likely understates the real scale, given that many loans taken out to buy stocks are labeled under other categories.

Meanwhile, domestic investors have poured ~$25.3 billion into South Korean shares year-to-date.

Retail investors in South Korea are aggressively rushing into stocks.

items.title: BREAKING: The SEC is set to release its so-called "innovation exemption" for tokenized stocks which will pave the path for trading digital versions of...
items.description_html:
BREAKING: The SEC is set to release its so-called "innovation exemption" for tokenized stocks which will pave the path for trading digital versions of securities, per Bloomberg.

Details include:

1. In a "surprise move," the SEC is leaning toward allowing the trading of tokenized assets

2. These tokenized assets would be tradeable on decentralized crypto platforms

3. The move could "reshape the landscape of the American stock market"

4. This would also be one of the US' biggest shifts into crypto infrastructure yet

Tokenized assets are rapidly expanding.

items.title: Shocking stat of the day: Semiconductor stocks have accounted for more than half of the S&P 500's +8% year-to-date gain, or +563 index points. NVIDIA,...
items.description_html:
Shocking stat of the day:

Semiconductor stocks have accounted for more than half of the S&P 500's +8% year-to-date gain, or +563 index points.

NVIDIA, $NVDA, alone has contributed +110 index points to the S&P 500.

This is followed by Micron, $MU, at +58 points, Broadcom, $AVGO, at +44 points, AMD, $AMD, at +40 points, and Intel, $INTC, at +39 points.

As a result, the remaining 495 stocks in the index have collectively contributed +272 points.

This comes as the Semiconductor index, $SOX, has rallied +64%, 8x the performance of the S&P 500.

Chip stocks now also account for 18% of the S&P 500’s market cap, near an all-time high.

Semiconductor stocks are a modern-day gold rush.

items.title: Investor risk appetite is surging at a historic pace: The ratio of levered long to short ETF trading volume is up to ~3.3, the highest since July 2024...
items.description_html:
Investor risk appetite is surging at a historic pace:

The ratio of levered long to short ETF trading volume is up to ~3.3, the highest since July 2024.

In other words, trading activity in levered long ETFs is more than 3 times larger than that of leveraged short ETFs.

This ratio has more than DOUBLED since late March and is comparable to the peak level seen during the 2020 market recovery following the pandemic crash.

The only other period that significantly exceeded the current level is the 2021 meme stock frenzy, when the indicator jumped to ~6.0.

Meanwhile, assets under management in US leveraged ETFs is up to a record $180 billion, rising ~$75 billion over the last 12 months.

Investors are extremely bullish right now.

items.title: BREAKING: Iran has sent an updated proposal for a deal to end the war, but the White House believes it is "insufficient for a deal," per Axios. Presid...
items.description_html:
BREAKING: Iran has sent an updated proposal for a deal to end the war, but the White House believes it is "insufficient for a deal," per Axios.

President Trump is now expected to convene his top national security team in the Situation Room on Tuesday to discuss military options.

A senior US official said that if Iran will not shift its position, the US will have to continue negotiations "through bombs."

Trump is reportedly considering resuming the Iran War due to Iran's rejection of many of his demands.

Peace talks continue to show little progress.

items.title: BREAKING: US CPI inflation is on track to exceed +5.0% as early as this year. Over the last 6 months, CPI inflation has averaged +0.4% on a MoM basis,...
items.description_html:
BREAKING: US CPI inflation is on track to exceed +5.0% as early as this year.

Over the last 6 months, CPI inflation has averaged +0.4% on a MoM basis, with March and April readings as high as +0.9% and +0.6%, respectively.

If this trend continues, this puts YoY inflation on pace to surge to +5.2% by the November midterms.

That would be the highest level since February 2023 and more than double the February 2026 print.

Even if monthly inflation prints ease to +0.3%, the YoY inflation rate would still rise to +4.4%, the highest since April 2023.

Inflation is back in full swing.

items.title: Hedge funds are all-in on semiconductor stocks: Semiconductor and semiconductor equipment stocks now account for 19% of total global hedge fund market...
items.description_html:
Hedge funds are all-in on semiconductor stocks:

Semiconductor and semiconductor equipment stocks now account for 19% of total global hedge fund market exposure, the highest on record.

This percentage has more than DOUBLED since the start of 2026.

By comparison, semiconductor stocks represented less than 2% of global hedge fund exposure during the 2022 bear market.

This comes as the Semiconductor index, $SOX, has rallied +405% over this period.

To put this into perspective, software and services stocks now account for ~2% of hedge fund portfolios.

This metric has declined -10 percentage points over the last 4 years.

Hedge funds are extremely bullish on chip stocks.

items.title: BREAKING: Iran has launched "Hormuz Safe," a Bitcoin-backed insurance service for shipping companies that want to transit the Strait of Hormuz. Detail...
items.description_html:
BREAKING: Iran has launched "Hormuz Safe," a Bitcoin-backed insurance service for shipping companies that want to transit the Strait of Hormuz.

Details include:

1. The Iranian government says it could generate more than $10 billion in revenue from the program

2. The service will be for "Iranian shipping companies and cargo owners"

3. "The shipment will be covered from the moment of confirmation and signed receipt will be given to the owner," Iran says

4. It is unclear if this insurance service will be charged in addition to tolls, which have been as much as $2 million per ship

Iran says an official website with more information is "coming soon."

items.title: BREAKING: The White House announces multiple US-China trade deals and developments following President Trump's meeting with China's President Xi. Deta...
items.description_html:
BREAKING: The White House announces multiple US-China trade deals and developments following President Trump's meeting with China's President Xi.

Details include:

1. China will address US concerns regarding supply chain shortages related to rare earths and other critical minerals

2. China has approved an initial purchase of 200 American-made Boeing aircraft for Chinese airlines

3. China will purchase at least $17 billion per year of US agricultural products in 2026, 2027, and 2028

4. China has restored market access for US beef by renewing expired listings of more than 400 US beef facilities and adding new listings

5. China has resumed imports of poultry from US states determined by the USDA to be free of highly pathogenic avian influenza

President Xi will be visiting the White House this fall.

items.title: BREAKING: The White House announces the establishment of the US-China Boards of Trade and Investment to "optimize the bilateral economic relationship"...
items.description_html:
BREAKING: The White House announces the establishment of the US-China Boards of Trade and Investment to "optimize the bilateral economic relationship" between China and the US.

The Board of Trade will allow the US Government and China to manage bilateral trade "across non-sensitive goods."

The Board of Investment will provide a government-to-government forum for discussing investment-related issues.

This announcement comes just days after the US-China summit.

items.title: It simply won't stop. It is Sunday night and the US 10Y Note Yield just casually hit 4.63%, the highest since February 2025. We are now ~4 basis point...
items.description_html:
It simply won't stop.

It is Sunday night and the US 10Y Note Yield just casually hit 4.63%, the highest since February 2025.

We are now ~4 basis points ABOVE the high that prompted President Trump's "90-day tariff pause" in April 2025.

This puts the 10Y Note Yield up +70 basis points since the Iran War, with US mortgage rates now nearing 7.00%+.

And, in a sudden turn of events, the odds of rate cuts have collapsed to 2% this year and US inflation is nearing 4%+.

The US bond market is collapsing in real-time.

Three names stand out consistently: Max Healthcare, Narayana Health, and Fortis.

items.title: Re Payor mix & CGHS – a quiet but powerful FY27+ earnings lever: 1) Max: ~25.6% H1 revenue from institutional; CGHS hike alone can add >₹200 cr reve...
items.description_html:
Re Payor mix & CGHS – a quiet but powerful FY27+ earnings lever:

1) Max: ~25.6% H1 revenue from institutional; CGHS hike alone can add >₹200 cr revenue with 85–90% EBITDA flow-through.

2) Medanta: CGHS+ECHS ~11–12% of revenue; 5–10% realization uplift in key procedures, but 7–9 months collection cycle.

3) KIMS: ~10% revenue from CGHS; ~20% price increase expected, adding ₹10–12 cr EBITDA next year.

4) Fortis: Govt mix 18–20% of revenue; sees rate hike as positive but cautious on 180-day receivable cycle.

5) Apollo: largely indifferent – CGHS still at ~65% discount vs insurance/cash; focus stays on cash/insurance (83% of IP revenue).

6) NH: schemes ~18% of India hospital revenue; expects 70–80% EBITDA flow-through from CGHS hike on incremental revenue.

In simple terms: Max, Fortis, NH, KIMS look like key CGHS beneficiaries.

items.title: Re Medanta – building a pan-India + MVT engine: 1) IP volumes +13% YoY; OP +15% YoY 2) “Developing hospitals” (Lucknow + Patna) revenue +28% YoY; L...
items.description_html:
Re Medanta – building a pan-India + MVT engine:

1) IP volumes +13% YoY; OP +15% YoY
2) “Developing hospitals” (Lucknow + Patna) revenue +28% YoY; Lucknow IP volume +30%
3) International revenue +49% YoY
4) Noida (550-bed) commissioning + Mumbai (750-bed planned) set up a strong medium-term growth runway

Management’s focus: more throughput from existing beds through ALOS reduction + complex services like robotics.

items.title: Re Max Healthcare – the “through-cycle” compounding machine: 1) 20th consecutive quarter of growth 2) Network gross revenue +21% YoY; occupied bed ...
items.description_html:
Re Max Healthcare – the “through-cycle” compounding machine:

1) 20th consecutive quarter of growth
2) Network gross revenue +21% YoY; occupied bed days +19% YoY, ARPOB +3%
3) Existing units: 14% like-for-like revenue growth, ~79% occupancy, 27.5% EBITDA margin (+90 bps YoY)
4) New units (Jaypee, Dwarka) contributed ₹144 cr revenue; Dwarka turned EBITDA positive in just 6 months.

Max has cracked the code on brownfield scale-up without margin collapse.

items.title: Re Fortis margin story: 1) Hospital margins up 150 bps YoY 2) Manesar turned EBITDA positive in <1 year; Greater Noida expected to hit ~15% margin in ...
items.description_html:
Re Fortis margin story:

1) Hospital margins up 150 bps YoY
2) Manesar turned EBITDA positive in <1 year; Greater Noida expected to hit ~15% margin in ~6 months
3) 13 facilities with >20% EBITDA margins now contribute 77% of hospital revenue
4) Oncology revenue +29% YoY; robotic surgeries +66% YoY

This is classic operating leverage + specialty mix at work.

items.title: Re Capacity expansion – who’s swinging the biggest bat? 1) Max Healthcare: Plans to add 8,300+ beds (vs ~5,200 now); ~4,800 beds over next 3–4 year...
items.description_html:
Re Capacity expansion – who’s swinging the biggest bat?

1) Max Healthcare: Plans to add 8,300+ beds (vs ~5,200 now); ~4,800 beds over next 3–4 years. NCR + Mumbai heavy.
2) Narayana Health (NH): moving towards 7,600+ beds with focused bets in Bangalore & Kolkata.
3) KIMS: 2,100+ beds in pipeline over already 6,114; South & West India focus.
4) Aster: targeting 7,800+ beds India; 10,360+ beds post QCIL merger.
5) Fortis: 550 beds already added in H1, another 300–400 beds in FY27 via brownfield/O&M.
6) Apollo: aiming 13% organic growth + 5% from new beds over 3 yrs; 6 new units across FY26/Q1FY27.
7) Medanta: Noida (550 beds) operational, Mumbai scaling to 750 beds, plus Guwahati, Ranchi, Patna, Delhi.

The capex cycle is real – but the question is: who’s adding beds without killing margins?

items.title: With Groww’s listing, Silicon Valley’s Y Combinator has cashed out $120 million — its first IPO exit in India! Y Combinator has backed over 200 sta...
items.description_html:
With Groww’s listing, Silicon Valley’s Y Combinator has cashed out $120 million — its first IPO exit in India!

Y Combinator has backed over 200 startups in India, with most of the deals between 2014 and 2022, typically involving investments of $120,000–150,000 each.

That amounts to roughly $32 million invested at the upper end in its seed portfolio.

Even assuming twice that amount was spent on follow-on rounds, the cashout at Groww’s IPO alone would be enough to return its total India investment.

Y Combinator still holds close to a 10% stake post-IPO, valued at over $900 million. Growth fund holds another 1.2% stake.

And its other portfolio companies, like Meesho, Zepto, and Razorpay, still have to go for an IPO.

Groww’s 1,400+ employees have created Rs 44 crore each in value at the IPO market capitalisation.

2× more than any other listed Indian internet company.

When discussing internet platforms, we often refer to network effects, margins, and operating leverage.

Groww runs one of the leanest teams among large venture-backed companies going for IPO, which is what makes it interesting.

items.title: At the start of the year, we identified that memory could become a near term and long term structural winner. Near term because Nvidia GPUs require HB...
items.description_html:
At the start of the year, we identified that memory could become a near term and long term structural winner.

Near term because Nvidia GPUs require HBM and global hyperscales are re-architecting a CPU-centric data center to a GPU-centric data center.

Cost of memory was going up as much if not more than the cost of GPUs. Long term because as foundational model cos make models more efficient, inference is likely to go on device which will structurally move up memory demand.

We identified SK Hynix and Micron as logical beneficiaries. Both these stocks are one of the best performing (if not the best performing) components of their country's frontline index.

Memory was the forgotten backwater of semis at the start of the year but is now a consensus trade.

Detailed Report - https://drive.google.com/file/d/1eM-rNRB38iLe2-FTxw5VbLG6uorlIons/view?usp=sharing

Disclaimer - Not a recommendation to buy/sell

items.title: Attended the renewable energy conference today. Key takeaways- 1. Most of the companies are talking about assembly of BESS, and not manufacturing of c...
items.description_html:
Attended the renewable energy conference today.

Key takeaways-

1. Most of the companies are talking about assembly of BESS, and not manufacturing of cells yet. As they are waiting for government duties.

2. Solar modules capacity is at 130GW and is going towards over capacity.

3. Solar cells stated capacity is at 20GW, but actual production is at 7-8 GW. As companies are finding it tough to scale, and ramp up production. Government might still go ahead with ALCM by June 2026.

4. Post solar cells, expansion into wafers and ALWM is a real possibility from 2028.

5. In terms of solar cells, many companies had high regards for Jupiter Solar in terms of setting the benchmark.

6. Waaree group is the first one to announce a mammoth capex in Lion cells. Let’s see how the profitability is for the business when it goes live.

7. Met couple of interesting proxies in Solar and Bess space. There’s an Indian company which is the world’s largest in dehumidifiers which are required for dry rooms. They even sell to China!

8. Solar park developers are working on the concept of Round the clock power. And they see a similar trend as USA might pick up here. Where IPPs do long term PPA with data centre cos for their power needs.

9. At the moment, Indian companies directly import cells from china and just assemble it into containers. Chinese costs are the lowest.

10. Companies in electrolytes and lithium salts had many foreign visitors at their booth. There could be big export demand for their product as China has. Even prohibited to sell Bess/battery materials to UsA under incentives for BESS. All of them mentioned that domestic Eco system might take 2-3 years to take off.

These were some of the key learning’s folks

items.title: My thoughts on the whole BESS situation The interesting part of a mega trend starting is at when does the sector hit an inflection point, where there ...
items.description_html:
My thoughts on the whole BESS situation

The interesting part of a mega trend starting is at when does the sector hit an inflection point, where there favourable unit economics, lack of commoditised pricing and tailwinds ( govt or global )

The interesting part about BESS is that @ishmohit1 also pointed out is the fact that there has to be a rate of fall in the BESS Prices , we have seen major contract repricing risk that can hurt the ecosystem in just few months , for me the real question to think about here is when this inflection point comes , trigger is govt policy with lesser pressure on pricing , till then we read agressively but don't act , waiting for the inflection point I think helps so we know that positive rate of change has come and come big , yes tailwinds are huge but so is the capital and competition , so we want to place our best bets and play our best hands only

This is a great time to keep the preparation ready and learn as much as you can about this space and then wait watch this space

items.title: China’s internal combustion car sales peaked in 2017 as electric vehicles took off— Electric cars have become incredibly popular in China. In 2020, ...
items.description_html:
China’s internal combustion car sales peaked in 2017 as electric vehicles took off—

Electric cars have become incredibly popular in China. In 2020, one in eighteen new cars sold was electric. By 2024, this had increased to one in two.

This growth has pushed down sales of internal combustion engine (ICE) cars, which run mostly on petrol. As you can see in the chart, sales of ICE cars peaked in 2017 and have declined since.

The world reached peak ICE car sales just one year later.

The displacement of petrol cars with electric ones is vital in decarbonizing transport. The rise of electric vehicles in China means the IEA expects oil demand to peak earlier than previously projected.

Here, “electric cars” include fully battery-electric ones and plug-in hybrids. In China, 56% of them were fully battery-electric.

(This Data Insight was written by @_HannahRitchie.)

items.description_html:
Something that really caught my ear while listening to an interview of Madhu Kela and Manish Chokhani yesterday with @_prashantnair , something that always will bother deep value thinkers

1) Large theme + start early + mediocre investment = Phenomenal gains

2) Exceptional investment + tailwind missing = Frustration

to quote Madhu Kela " I have come out very frustrated in investing in best companies where the tailwind was missing"

Something to ponder about , it is more variable change of attention that we should give to rate of change +tailwind and not only best player , this I speak in terms of an equity shareholder not a promoter , promoter will continue to benefit by running a great company with headwinds , but an investor wont , as incremental % of profit will be difficult to capture in contrast to a company that is in tailwinds but mediocre ( with base decent management, may not be best , but good ) but rate of change is visible

Would love to hear your take on this @chokhani_manish

items.title: Re The AI thematic is definitively a structural buildout, not a bubble, tracing the arc of the Shale revolution from scarcity to abundance. The invest...
items.description_html:
Re The AI thematic is definitively a structural buildout, not a bubble, tracing the arc of the Shale revolution from scarcity to abundance.

The investment cycle is pivoting decisively from Phase 1 Infrastructure (chips and data centre buildout) to Phase 2 Implementation, where winners are defined by quantifiable return on investment (ROI). The key tension shifts from raw compute supply to effective utilization.

The physical bottleneck of Power/Grid capacity remains the near-term moat for core infrastructure and electrical equipment providers. But asymmetric upside now resides in companies that leverage AI for hard-to-replicate workflows or own durable data flywheels, protecting them from the 30–40% annual decline in inference costs driven by open-source commoditization. The next frontier is Physical Automation/Robotics, offering secular growth at cyclical valuations.

The era of merely owning "picks and shovels" is maturing; the time for granular, domain-specific investment in sticky applications and power availability has arrived.

items.description_html:
Re Just quickly listing out some of the proxy plays - Picking out the shovel that is digging the gold

> Connectors & Optics Theme: Connectors/Optics Modules. High-speed data transfer (800G to 1.6T) necessitates fiber components with high margins. Co: Fujikura (5803.T). Thesis: High Operating Profit Margin (OPM) beneficiary of GPU cross-connects increasing usage of optical multi-core connectors. Evidence: Holds high market share in multi-core optical cables/connectors for DC/AI server apps

> Power Supply Theme: AI Rack Power Supply. High power density requires specialized, high-share vendors. Co: Delta Electronics (http://2308.TW). Thesis: Dominant supplier capturing the high-density AI server rack power market. Evidence: Estimated ~50% global supply share for server power supply; holds ~75% share for AI server rack power supply. Co: TD Power . Thesis: Dominant player in turbine for Gas powered Engines. Evidence: Supplying to all the 3 OEMs amongst the few turbine suppliers across the globe compounding sales at 20% and Profit at ~50% CAGR in the last 5 years

> Power Management Theme: Secure Power/Electrical Equipment. Electrification equipment scales directly with rising wattages. Co: Eaton (ETN). Thesis: Key play on DC equipment growth; providing MV switchgear and 3-phase UPS. Evidence: Highlighted in global coverage as key play on DC equipment theme.

> Liquid/Hybrid Cooling Systems. Mandatory shift due to heat generated by high-density racks. Co: Vertiv (VRT). Thesis: Pure-play exposure to liquid cooling transition (40% CAGR to 2028) driven by high-power chips (>1kW). Evidence: Vertiv mentioned as providing cooling solutions in DC stack context.

> Gensets & EPC Theme: Backup Power/Grid Construction. Essential due to utility infrastructure constraints/power availability issues. Co: Cummins (CMI), Deere (DE). Thesis (CMI): Benefiting from high demand for backup DG sets for increased power stability/availability. Thesis (DE): Heavy machinery OEM required for ground clearing, site logistics in Stargate-scale DC buildouts.

> Colocation/DC REIT Theme: Data Center Real Estate. Capturing high power density demand (30kW+/rack). Co: Equinix (EQIX). Thesis: Global leader monetizing infra; capturing high demand from hyperscalers seeking flexibility and agility. Evidence: Example DC service provider benefiting from high computing demand; 12% global market share.

I have attached here the list of companies that might be the potential supplier of one of the largest DC projects “Stargate” - https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1ZFXyMEjWyQbkAflRBLgrkS0sZ7HwdMgZ/edit?usp=sharing&ouid=118333742077224865973&rtpof=true&sd=true

items.title: Re Evolv Technologies is a prime example of the specialized integration described in your note - It fits this third phase of AI by: Deeply integrating...
items.description_html:
Re Evolv Technologies is a prime example of the specialized integration described in your note -

It fits this third phase of AI by:

Deeply integrating AI into a specific industry: Evolv applies advanced AI, including sensor fusion and computer vision, directly to the physical security and public safety sector.

Demonstrating high specialization: Instead of developing general AI, Evolv focuses on a single, critical application: real-time threat detection. This showcases the trend of companies concentrating on niche AI subsets to drive targeted advancements.

Acting as an autonomous agent: The Evolv Express® system autonomously executes the task of screening individuals and distinguishing between threats and benign items, making it an "agent" that performs a specialized function with minimal human intervention.

More such companies innovating into this specialised AI are present in the tables below

items.title: Re Layer 3 - Applications & Agentic AI The third phase signifies a deeper integration of AI and ML across various industries and societal aspects, alo...
items.description_html:
Re Layer 3 - Applications & Agentic AI

The third phase signifies a deeper integration of AI and ML across various industries and societal aspects, alongside increased specialization within the AI field itself. During this period, the definition of "artificial intelligence" may evolve beyond current Large Language Models (LLMs), with "AI assistants" or "agents" capable of autonomous task execution becoming prevalent.

AI and ML technologies are becoming ubiquitous, impacting sectors such as healthcare (for medical imaging and disease prediction), finance (for fraud detection and financial forecasting), and transportation (for self-driving cars and route optimization).

Concurrently, AI research is on a way of becoming more specialized. Some researchers and companies will concentrate on specific AI subsets like natural language processing, computer vision, or reinforcement learning, while others will focus on particular applications or industries. This specialization will foster greater expertise and drive more targeted advancements.

Key advancements in this phase include accelerated computing, edge AI, and explainable AI, all of which will enhance the capabilities and extend the reach of AI technologies. Future directions for AI development include continual learning, AI alignment, and the integration of common-sense reasoning into AI systems.

items.title: Re Layer 2 - Models & Frameworks What A simple way to think here is consumer facing applications is an easy part of the game and now slowly is becomin...
items.description_html:
Re Layer 2 - Models & Frameworks What

A simple way to think here is consumer facing applications is an easy part of the game and now slowly is becoming more of a commodity but what might be the moat here is building LLMs

Where products like ChatGPT, Claude, Perplexity, manus etc. are applications but they are largely dependent on the LLM models they are built upon and these LLMs are difficult to build and enhance. So it is obvious for the competition to be high in applications as the LLMs are getting democratised. Companies involved in building LLMs are META, GOOGLE, Anthropic, Deepseek, etc.

This second phase focuses on making AI and ML widely accessible. Building on the infrastructure established in the first phase, major players and new companies will refine and develop the technology. New applications introduced to consumers and businesses, with some becoming essential and others failing and everyday there might be a new challenger here or there.

A turning point reached when these tools become both accessible and intuitive for the average consumer, potentially leading to a productivity surge similar to the late 1990s. During this time, major tech companies will continue to expand their cloud-based AI services, offering affordable access to powerful computational resources without the need for new data centers. This will significantly reduce barriers for startups and individual developers. This phase can run concurrently with the first, but the first phase remains the limiting factor until sufficient infrastructure is built out.

items.title: Re Layer 1: Data centres has to be the base layer of things Data centers, housing servers with powerful GPUs and specialized accelerators like TPUs, F...
items.description_html:
Re Layer 1: Data centres has to be the base layer of things

Data centers, housing servers with powerful GPUs and specialized accelerators like TPUs, FPGAs, and ASICs, are the foundational backbone for machine learning computations. The maturation of distributed computing technologies will further enhance this, enabling the combined processing power of networked machines to tackle large-scale machine learning tasks. This will also incentivize portfolios heavily invested in advanced networking solutions.

Beyond hardware, the initial phase also saw significant advancements in machine learning software. This included the development and refinement of platforms and frameworks such as TensorFlow, PyTorch, and Keras. These tools simplify the development and implementation of machine learning models for engineers and researchers by abstracting away the complexities of underlying computations and hardware. Ultimately, this foundational phase (Phase 1) is a critical bottleneck for subsequent phases (Phase 2 and 3), as robust computing potential is essential for all other developments.

Names: Equinix (12% global share), Digital Realty, Vertiv, Thermaltake. KPIs: MW Capacity, Power Density (moving to >30kW/rack vs. 8-10kW traditional).

items.title: Re Here also the market is controlled by the triads - SK Hynix and Samsung (Korean players) and Micron (American player) together they control close t...
items.description_html:
Re Here also the market is controlled by the triads - SK Hynix and Samsung (Korean players) and Micron (American player) together they control close to 80% market share

SK Hynix leads the AI memory market, surpassing Samsung, thanks to its early development of High Bandwidth Memory (HBM) starting in 2013.

HBM, which stacks DRAM chips for faster data transfer, was crucial for AI. SK Hynix's superior MR-MUF manufacturing process resulted in a more reliable product, making it a key supplier for Nvidia's AI chips.

Micron is also one of the players that has been selected for the HBM chips or Nvidia’s H200 tensor core chips.

Samsung, focused on high-volume, low-cost memory, was slow to adapt to the AI shift, costing it significant revenue as it struggled to qualify its HBM chips with Nvidia.

items.title: Re Memory has become the new frontier for AI because it directly addresses the most significant limiting factor in AI performance today. For decades, ...
items.description_html:
Re Memory has become the new frontier for AI because it directly addresses the most significant limiting factor in AI performance today.

For decades, memory chips like DRAM (Dynamic Random-Access Memory) were the unglamorous, low-margin part of the semiconductor industry. They were treated as a commodity, where the primary goal was to produce them as cheaply as possible. The real value and innovation were seen in processor chips (CPUs and GPUs) from companies like Nvidia and Intel.

The new thesis, driven entirely by the AI revolution, is that memory is no longer a commodity but a critical performance bottleneck. Large Language Models (LLMs) are incredibly data-hungry and have run into the "memory wall"—a point where even the fastest processor is left idle, waiting for data to be retrieved from memory. This has flipped the industry on its head. Now, specialized, high-performance memory is the key to unlocking the next level of AI capability, and customers are prioritizing performance and power over cost.

items.title: Re Cooling & Interconnect Shift High-performance compute drives infrastructural change: Air cooling becomes unsuitable when single chip power exceeds ...
items.description_html:
Re Cooling & Interconnect Shift High-performance compute drives infrastructural change: Air cooling becomes unsuitable when single chip power exceeds 1kW. The liquid cooling market is expected to grow at a 40% CAGR, reaching $3.5bn by 2028 (30% market share, up from 10% currently). InfiniBand (NVDA technology) dominates high-bandwidth, low-latency connectivity for training/inference. Optics demand surges over fiber/cable infrastructure.

Our discussions suggest that Immersion Cooling will likely achieve a market share higher than the 3-4% recently projected by Legrand (compared to the current approximately 1%).

items.title: Re Capex Scale The buildout scale is staggering. Average data centre projects are now over 300MW, with talk of 1GW campuses. Hyperscaler DC Capex (Alp...
items.description_html:
Re Capex Scale The buildout scale is staggering. Average data centre projects are now over 300MW, with talk of 1GW campuses. Hyperscaler DC Capex (Alphabet, AWS, Microsoft, Meta) combined total for 2025E is projected significantly over $100bn. GPU demand continues:

Vertiv estimates GPU 10-year growth CAGR of 35-60%. for accelerated computing transition. In addition, server manufacturers are packing more CPUs and GPUs into each rack to meet the accelerating demand for high-performance computing, further increasing per-rack density.

The rapid growth leads to increased power density requirement as AI data centre power requirements could be 4-5x vs. current data centres.

items.title: Re Infra vs. Dot-Com The key difference: Hyperscalers are profitable. Today's infrastructure buildout is marked by truly capital-intensive spending, u...
items.description_html:
Re Infra vs. Dot-Com

The key difference: Hyperscalers are profitable. Today's infrastructure buildout is marked by truly capital-intensive spending, unlike the early internet build. The build is constrained by power, not by demand.

Hyperscalers (MSFT, GOOGL, AMZN) are model providers who will spend billions on hardware/DC infrastructure. This is a fundamental capacity being laid down for AI training and inference.

The shale revolution was a resounding success, fundamentally altering the global energy landscape. New drilling and fracking technologies transformed the United States into the leading producer of oil and gas, lessening dependence on the Middle East and reinforcing American influence. However, most investors in shale ultimately faced financial ruin. The very productivity gains attracted an excess of capital, leading to a market flooded by debt-fueled independent producers. Even OPEC's efforts to quash shale only accelerated this cycle. The irony is that while shale's abundance achieved geopolitical triumph, it simultaneously deprived capital of returns.

Artificial Intelligence (AI) appears to be following a similar trajectory of abundance. Each new GPU cluster, every energized megawatt, and every advancement in quantization or inference technology contributes to this trend.

The key takeaway is not that success is fleeting, but that success does not guarantee profits for everyone. Returns in AI will materialize, but primarily for those positioned within the protective framework currently being established around it.

items.title: Re Power is the Margin of Safety The critical bottleneck is tangible: Power Availability. Bloomberg NEF forecasts data-center power consumption will m...
items.description_html:
Re Power is the Margin of Safety

The critical bottleneck is tangible: Power Availability. Bloomberg NEF forecasts data-center power consumption will more than triple by 2035. This digestion risk creates a high-margin floor for infrastructure companies, insulating them from immediate software commoditization risks seen in past cycles

Let’s understand that why we are saying that AI is a game of scale rather than just building applications -

For example of you consider LLAMA 3.1 model made by meta which is trained on 405B parameters i.e in Pre-training period itself it has to operate 38 septillion math operations that is a lot of math to do which would require ~4486 years with one H100 GPU which cost around $25-40k a piece and to train this one Meta used 16000 GPUs and the process is explained in this video in an interesting way - https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=CrJJPlRO9bI

items.title: Is AI the next IT bubble? Short answer “No”. AI is tracing the arc of Shales. Like the US oil boom, the narrative is shifting from scarcity to abund...
items.description_html:
Is AI the next IT bubble?

Short answer “No”. AI is tracing the arc of Shales. Like the US oil boom, the narrative is shifting from scarcity to abundance. The early parallels—surging capital, stretched valuations—echo the dot-com era. But unlike 1999–00 (where productivity gains evaporated) or the 1980s AI Winter (rule-based systems failed), this is a profound, capital-intensive structural buildout.

Here in this thread I would not bore you with all the story of how AI was built and history of it rather would jump on straight to the discussion on how we view this as the multi year boom and where does the real value lies in the AI value chain out there

So let’s dive deep and understand what AI has brought in for us investors as an opportunity…

items.title: Over the last 60 years, the world population has more than doubled. This has inevitably reduced the land available per person to live and grow food. H...
items.description_html:
Over the last 60 years, the world population has more than doubled.

This has inevitably reduced the land available per person to live and grow food.

How have we managed to feed a rapidly growing population with ever-shrinking land resources?

Let's look at this for cereals, which make up more than half of total caloric intake in many countries and dominate global arable land use by area.

We see in the chart that for cereals, this has been achieved by massively increasing the yield — the world can now produce more than three times as much cereal from a given area of land as it did in 1961 (i.e., an increase in yield of 214%).

In the same period, land used for cereal has only increased by 14%.

This increase in yield output (or "intensification") has typically been achieved through a combination of chemical inputs (such as fertilizer, pesticides, and herbicides); improved water use (e.g., irrigation); mechanization and improved farming practices; and the use of higher-yielding crop strains or seeds.

items.title: Declining child mortality, fast and slow— As this chart shows, two centuries ago, about one in three children in Sweden died before they were five ye...
items.description_html:
Declining child mortality, fast and slow—

As this chart shows, two centuries ago, about one in three children in Sweden died before they were five years old.

Since then, the child mortality rate in Sweden has declined to 0.3%.

South Korea achieved a similar reduction much faster.

This is often the case: the first countries to improve living conditions usually needed much longer than some of those countries catching up later — the latter can learn from what worked elsewhere.

(This Data Insight was written by @MaxCRoser.)

items.title: Does the news reflect what we die from? More than 80% of people — including surveyed Americans, Brits, Germans, and Italians — say they follow the n...
items.description_html:
Does the news reflect what we die from?

More than 80% of people — including surveyed Americans, Brits, Germans, and Italians — say they follow the news because they “want to know what is going on in the world around them.”

It’s not just that people expect the news to inform them about what’s going on in the world. Most think that it does. When asked what emotions the news generates, “informed” was the most common response.

This is what media outlets themselves promise to do. Here are several quotes from the New York Times’s mission statement:

“We seek the truth and help people understand the world. [...]

We help a global audience understand a vast and diverse world.”

However, as we discuss in a new article, the media focuses on a particular sliver of our world, leaving much of the “vast and diverse world” largely out of their reporting. We investigate this through the lens of health, looking at causes of death and reporting in the United States.

Our point is not that we should want or expect the media’s coverage to perfectly match the real distribution of deaths, although we’d argue that it would be better if it were less skewed.

We wrote this article so that you, the reader, are aware of a significant disconnect between what we often hear and what actually happens.

It’s easy to conflate what we see in the news with the reality of our world, and keeping this mismatch in mind can help you avoid falling into this trap.

items.title: Re The comprehensive review of Q1 FY26 confirms that the Indian hospital sector is navigating a crucial phase, marked by unprecedented quantitative ex...
items.description_html:
Re The comprehensive review of Q1 FY26 confirms that the Indian hospital sector is navigating a crucial phase, marked by unprecedented quantitative expansion underpinned by a critical shift towards qualitative clinical and corporate leadership.

The core trend is clear: industry momentum is robust, driven by structural tailwinds resulting in a projected 9–11% CAGR through FY27 and aggressive capacity additions, exemplified by Apollo Hospitals (AHEL) targeting 13,750+ approved beds by FY30

For investors, the takeaway for Q2 onwards is unambiguous: look beyond headline revenue growth and focus intensely on execution. The winners will be those who consistently demonstrate superior Return on Capital Employed (ROCE), strong topline growth and faster Ebitda growth

The Indian hospital sector is transitioning from a volume story to a value story—where longevity, governance, clinical excellence, and disciplined capital deployment are the true measures of enduring market leadership.

items.title: Re Capacity Expansion Pipeline All major players have aggressive bed addition plans, focusing heavily on India’s high-demand regions: > AHEL: Approve...
items.description_html:
Re Capacity Expansion Pipeline

All major players have aggressive bed addition plans, focusing heavily on India’s high-demand regions:

> AHEL: Approved capacity expansion for 3,600+ beds over the next 5 years. Total planned beds by FY30 over 13,750.
> MAXHEALTH: Planning roughly 2x bed capacity (from ~5,200 currently) in the next 4–5 years.
> ASTER/QCIL: Total planned capacity expansion of 2,600+ beds for ASTER alone, reaching 7,800+ total capacity. QCIL also plans about 1,200 beds.
> MEDANTA: Broader expansion pipeline includes 2,000 beds over the course of three to four years.
> JLHL: Total bed capacity targeted at ~2,500 beds.

items.title: Re Future Growth Guidance > AHEL: Targeting organic hospital growth of 13%–14% on existing assets, with additional growth from new beds. Aiming for 2...
items.description_html:
Re Future Growth Guidance

> AHEL: Targeting organic hospital growth of 13%–14% on existing assets, with additional growth from new beds. Aiming for 25%+ margin on existing hospitals.
> MAXHEALTH: Expects margin percentage to decrease due to mix shift (higher complexity, international business) but overall EBITDA per bed and ROCE will increase.
> FORTIS: Expects margin momentum to continue (Hospital EBITDA Q1 FY26: 22.1%).
> YATHARTH: Looking for 8%–10% ARPOB growth for FY26 and FY27 on a blended basis. Targets group EBITDA margins to remain similar to Q4 FY25 (24.6%).
> HCG: Expects margin to reach at least 20% (low 20s) this year.

> ASTER/QCIL: CONGO share increased 210 basis points to 58.4% for QCIL. ASTER’s Oncology share reached 11% (up from 9% Q1 FY24).
> YATHARTH: Oncology contributes 10% to Group revenues.
> MEDANTA: Cancer contribution to revenue mix is increasing

items.title: Re Rainbow Children's Medicare Rainbow is India's largest chain focused on comprehensive pediatric care and obstetrics. Q1 FY26 Revenue grew 7%, and P...
items.description_html:
Re Rainbow Children's Medicare

Rainbow is India's largest chain focused on comprehensive pediatric care and obstetrics. Q1 FY26 Revenue grew 7%, and PAT surged 35% YoY.

ARPOB grew significantly (13% YoY to ₹63,323). The management noted that volume growth in mature hospitals was muted due to a delayed onset of the pediatric season.

Expansion is planned in Gurugram (by September 2027) and Coimbatore.

items.title: Re Krishna Institute of Medical Sciences KIMS is actively absorbing the impact of ramping up five new hospitals, which contributed ₹21 crores in comb...
items.description_html:
Re Krishna Institute of Medical Sciences

KIMS is actively absorbing the impact of ramping up five new hospitals, which contributed ₹21 crores in combined losses in Q1 FY26.

The Telangana mature cluster expects moderate volume growth of 5-6% due to near-peak capacity.

KIMS is focused on asset-light O&M contracts (e.g., Sangli and Guntur) which have broken even and are generating top line revenue.

items.title: Re Yatharth Hospitals Yatharth achieved its highest ever quarterly revenue and profitability in Q1 FY26. ARPOB reached ₹32,395 (+6% YoY). New hospita...
items.description_html:
Re Yatharth Hospitals

Yatharth achieved its highest ever quarterly revenue and profitability in Q1 FY26. ARPOB reached ₹32,395 (+6% YoY).

New hospitals in Model Town, Delhi (300 beds, inaugurated July 2025) and Greater Faridabad (400 beds, operational August 2025) are expected to accelerate growth from Q2 onwards, adding ~700 beds.

These new NCR units are expected to have an ARPOB exceeding the group average.

items.title: Re Jupiter Life Line Hospitals JLHL reported a 20.5% growth in Total Income in Q1 FY26. ARPOB stood at ₹67,300. The expansion strategy focuses on thr...
items.description_html:
Re Jupiter Life Line Hospitals

JLHL reported a 20.5% growth in Total Income in Q1 FY26. ARPOB stood at ₹67,300.

The expansion strategy focuses on three new greenfield hospitals in underserved yet densely populated metro locations: Dombivli (expected Q1 FY27 commissioning), Mira-Bhayandar, and Bibvewadi (Pune).

The Dombivli hospital is expected to incur negative EBITDA in its first year, causing slight consolidated compression next year.

items.title: Re Narayana Hrudayalaya NH continues to expand its Integrated Care business (NHIC and NHIL), pivoting towards holistic patient management and insuranc...
items.description_html:
Re Narayana Hrudayalaya

NH continues to expand its Integrated Care business (NHIC and NHIL), pivoting towards holistic patient management and insurance offerings, with Aditi Health Insurance expanding to multiple cities.

The Bangalore cluster accounted for 36% of hospital operating revenues.

The Integrated Care business (CIHL) (Cayman) reported revenue of $9.6 million and an EBITDA of $2.1 million. Management has stated that the margin profile of the core Cayman hospital business remains stable and largely unchanged.

The company is maintaining its growth trajectory despite capacity constraints in Bangalore and Kolkata through digitization and automation. The expansion pipeline includes 7,690 beds.

Cayman Islands Strategy: The market is viewed as the broader Caribbean region, not just the local population. The new hospital and integrated care businesses are in a ramp-up phase and are performing better than planned.

items.title: Re Global Health (Medanta) Medanta demonstrated continued strong performance, with Total Income growing 19% YoY to ₹10,513 million. The strategy reli...
items.description_html:
Re Global Health (Medanta)

Medanta demonstrated continued strong performance, with Total Income growing 19% YoY to ₹10,513 million.

The strategy relies on two distinct verticals: Mature hospitals (Gurugram) and Developing hospitals (Lucknow, Patna).

Developing hospitals exhibited strong growth, with revenue up 35.9% and EBITDA up 59.8% YoY in Q1 FY26. The Noida hospital (550 beds) is preparing for phase I operations (~300 beds) in Q2 FY26

items.title: Re Fortis Healthcare Fortis reported Q1 FY26 consolidated revenue growth of 16.6% (₹2,167 Cr), with the Hospital business growing 18.6%. Hospital Ope...
items.description_html:
Re Fortis Healthcare

Fortis reported Q1 FY26 consolidated revenue growth of 16.6% (₹2,167 Cr), with the Hospital business growing 18.6%.

Hospital Operating EBITDA margin improved significantly to 22.1%. Growth was driven by a 10.2% YoY increase in ARPOB (₹2.65 Cr p.a.) and a 7.8% increase in occupied beds.

Fortis expanded its network via an O&M agreement with Gleneagles India to manage operations of ~700 beds across five hospitals.

items.title: Re Max Healthcare MAXHEALTH delivered a strong start to FY26 with 27% YoY revenue growth. The Existing Units showed robust performance, reaching aroun...
items.description_html:
Re Max Healthcare

MAXHEALTH delivered a strong start to FY26 with 27% YoY revenue growth.

The Existing Units showed robust performance, reaching around 80% occupancy (excluding recent additions).

The focus metric is annualized EBITDA per bed, which stood at ₹68 lakhs for the Network.

Expansion plans include Nanavati and Smart Saket opening phases in Q3 FY26, supporting the target to double bed capacity in the next 4–5 years.

items.title: Re Apollo hospitals AHEL reported consolidated revenue growth of 15% YoY in Q1 FY26 (₹5,842 crore). The company is India’s largest integrated health...
items.description_html:
Re Apollo hospitals

AHEL reported consolidated revenue growth of 15% YoY in Q1 FY26 (₹5,842 crore).

The company is India’s largest integrated healthcare ecosystem. Strategic capacity expansion targets 13,750+ approved beds by FY30.

The company is proceeding with the strategic reorganisation/demerger of Apollo HealthCo (Omnichannel pharmacy and digital health), which targets a revenue run-rate of ₹25,000 crore by the end of FY27 with 7% EBITDA margins on a combined basis

items.title: Re Expansion Strategy: Metros and Asset-Light Models Hospitals are deploying multi-pronged expansion strategies, prioritizing metros due to higher per...
items.description_html:
Re Expansion Strategy: Metros and Asset-Light Models

Hospitals are deploying multi-pronged expansion strategies, prioritizing metros due to higher per capita income and advanced talent pools.

MAXHEALTH has 76% of its beds in metros. The trend favours asset-light models, brownfield expansions, and Operations and Maintenance (O&M) contracts for faster ramp-up and better capital efficiency.

KIMS, ASTER are heavily utilizing leased and O&M models for rapid expansion

items.title: Re These entities leverage scale, brand equity, and diversified service offerings. Max Healthcare stands out with industry-leading operational metrics...
items.description_html:
Re These entities leverage scale, brand equity, and diversified service offerings.

Max Healthcare stands out with industry-leading operational metrics, including a Q1 FY26 network occupancy of ~76%, an ARPOB of ₹78,200, and a ROCE of ~26%.

Its strategy is focused on deepening its presence in the high-potential Delhi NCR and Mumbai markets. Apollo Hospitals, the largest player by network size, is executing a significant pan-India expansion of over 3,500 beds.

A key value-unlocking catalyst for Apollo is its HealthCo subsidiary, which combines a vast pharmacy network with a digital platform and is on track for profitability.

items.title: Re The Rise of Super Specialisation: Oncology and CONGO The shift towards specialty care is pronounced, driven by the increasing burden of non-communi...
items.description_html:
Re The Rise of Super Specialisation: Oncology and CONGO

The shift towards specialty care is pronounced, driven by the increasing burden of non-communicable diseases (NCDs), which accounted for nearly 62% of all deaths in India in 2016.

This fuels the growth of high-value specialties such as Cardiology, Oncology, Neurosciences, and Orthopaedics (CONGO).

Several players, including MAXHEALTH and HCG, are aggressively expanding their Oncology offerings, viewing it as a major structural growth driver

Source - JM Financials

items.title: France opened a flurry of nuclear power plants in the 1980s and 1990s, giving it low-carbon electricity ever since— At the turn of the millennium, Fr...
items.description_html:
France opened a flurry of nuclear power plants in the 1980s and 1990s, giving it low-carbon electricity ever since—

At the turn of the millennium, France had one of the lowest-carbon electricity grids in Europe (and the world).

While countries like the UK and Germany emitted well over 500 grams of CO₂ per kilowatt-hour of electricity, France emitted just 80 grams — six times less. This was mostly thanks to nuclear power.

In the 1980s and 1990s, France rapidly expanded its power grid, and almost all of this growth came from new nuclear plants. The chart shows this: in the 1980s alone, nuclear power grew from 60 to over 300 terawatt-hours.

By 2000, nuclear power supplied almost 80% of the country’s electricity, making it much cleaner than its neighbors, mostly relying on coal and gas.

France still has one of the cleanest grids in Europe, although it has added very little nuclear power in the 21st century. It has opened just one plant in the last 25 years, in Flamanville, following long delays and cost overruns.

In the last decade, solar and wind power have grown the most.

(This Data Insight was written by @_HannahRitchie.)

items.title: Don't let the no rate cut from RBI today fool you ! Lots of important regulatory stuff.. all aimed at easing.. 10 quick things. 1. Banks can finance M...
items.description_html:
Don't let the no rate cut from RBI today fool you !

Lots of important regulatory stuff.. all aimed at easing..

10 quick things.

1. Banks can finance M&A - basically lend more freely against shares/REITs/InvITs

2. "Overlap" bar removed in bank–group business rules - Gives bank boards/NOFHCs room to structure group entities better

3. Basel III capital charge update – Draft norms coming & aim is to capital rules more risk-sensitive

4 . Shift to Expected Credit Loss (ECL) provisioning - move away from incurred-loss model

5. Deposit insurance premium - Stronger banks pay less

6. NFBCs - Risk weights - To be better aligned with project stage (lower for operational projs)

7. Withdrawal of 2016 large borrower guidelines

8. Discussion paper for granting new UCB (urban co-operative banks) licences

9. 250 master directions to replace scattered circulars

10. Easing restrictions on transaction accounts – Current, OD, & cash credit account rules to be relaxed

#StockMarket #Nifty #BankNifty #rbipolicy #rbimonetarypolicy #India

items.title: Re Regional archetypes (cheat-sheet): > North – structurally strong, mature consolidation, rational pricing. > West – saturated but healthy; seaborn...

Re Regional archetypes (cheat-sheet):

> North – structurally strong, mature consolidation, rational pricing.
> West – saturated but healthy; seaborne logistics lower freight.
> East – volume fiesta and supply fiesta; abundant fly-ash/slag.
> South – capacity jungle, leadership fluid; discipline improving.
> Central – steady demand but gets “dumped” from all sides.

items.title: Re What changed under the hood? The shape of this upcycle is price-led + consolidation-led (capacity broadly tracking demand), unlike the 2000s “dema...
items.description_html:
Re What changed under the hood?

The shape of this upcycle is price-led + consolidation-led (capacity broadly tracking demand), unlike the 2000s “demand > supply” boom.

That means the sector’s P&L fixes will come from mix/price and cost architecture, not just a volume wave.

Simply the P&L in this cycle would move more from the bottom line fixes rather than topline demand push. Topline growth might just track just the industry growth rates

items.title: Re Why now? Prices held firm into FY26 despite an early monsoon. Historically, monsoon months don’t support hikes; this time companies attempted hike...
items.description_html:
Re Why now? Prices held firm into FY26 despite an early monsoon.

Historically, monsoon months don’t support hikes; this time companies attempted hikes and realisations stayed better than FY24–25 (West is the outlier).

That’s a signal of tightening structure, not a one-off.

items.title: Northern arc Capital , when Ashish was asked by Varinder (@varinder_bansal ) about what the management thinks about the company not getting proper cov...
items.description_html:
Northern arc Capital , when Ashish was asked by Varinder (@varinder_bansal ) about what the management thinks about the company not getting proper coverage or market understanding its business model or understanding what the market misses to understand in their business ,

instead of talking about the market or pointing out some irrationality or coverage and peers , he chose to answer by actually pointing out positive track record of the company across cycles and humbly responded with the impact their company has created over the last decade to those underserved

Great answer in my opinion , market will eventually reward performance , but to look at this question in this way tells a lot about strong or rather good management quality , numbers follow intent , never the other way around

( disc: biased here so take my reading with a pinch of salt , no financial reco )

items.title: A few months back at , we studied a unique auto ancillaries that has been consistently able to outperform the auto growth. A business whose business m...
items.description_html:
A few months back at , we studied a unique auto ancillaries that has been consistently able to outperform the auto growth.

A business whose business model who thrives on the concept that, "Its much easier and cheaper for the OEMs to make the cars look innovative that them to be actually innovative"

SJS Enterprises is a quiet beneficiary of this shift sitting at the intersection of Good Products, Deep Pocket Penetration, and Rising Aesthetics.

If cars are turning into lifestyle statements, SJS is the ideal proxy to this :
> from decals, overlays & chrome plating → to 3D lux parts & hydrographics
> from low-ASP commoditised trims → to high-margin premium surfaces

This is a sustainable long term trend and just an one-off fad led by premiumisation - global & domestic.

Consumers want aspirational interiors & Deep pocket penetration - once inside an OEM platform, SJS expands wallet share model-by-model and by increasing ticket size and launching more products with similar synergies

This is not just a story but a reality which the company has been able to show even into the numbers by outperforming the Industry for 23 consecutive quarters.

Disclaimer - not a recommendation to buy/sell
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=5Lb2ZDK_PlI

items.title: One thing to keep in mind for the PEB ( pre engineered buildings ) sector now is changing capital dynamics , there have been 3 players that have come ...
items.description_html:
One thing to keep in mind for the PEB ( pre engineered buildings ) sector now is changing capital dynamics , there have been 3 players that have come in with a IPO over the last year
Interarch , M&B , epack prefab( total 1754 crores raised combined )

Now what does this say about everyone in the sector heading towards leaner balance sheets and competition , yes market demand is high and that will keep everything going strong until orderbook buildup is strong , and I do own interarch and pennar from much lower levels ( 1180 and 144) and both are doing very well and I am still positive on them , but now industry competition will be a factor as few players are flushed with cash , for now this competition is not a worry because demand outlook is strong , but going forward these dynamics on orderbook only become even more important to track and watch closely

Just a variable to keep in mind, no worrying signs as of yet , but something that I will watch !

I had asked the management of Pennar as well on what is their take on this , and this is what they had said

@ishmohit1 @soicfinance @EquityInsightss

( disc: invested and biased in pennar at 144 , and interarch at 1180 from IPO , no fresh reco, both have done well for us from public initiation , no fin advice)

items.title: Californians now travel millions of miles each month in driverless taxis— After only two years, California’s driverless taxis now transport passenge...
items.description_html:
Californians now travel millions of miles each month in driverless taxis—

After only two years, California’s driverless taxis now transport passengers for more than four million miles per month. Although they still make up only a fraction of taxi trips in the state, they are expanding quickly.

This chart shows the monthly distance traveled in driverless trips in California. It measures the total number of passenger-miles, summing up the distance traveled by all passengers.

In August 2023, California regulators fully approved self-driving taxi services in San Francisco for companies Cruise and Waymo. However, Cruise stopped operating in late 2023 due to safety and regulatory issues, so the recent growth reflects only Waymo’s service.

Trips stayed under half a million miles per month until mid-2024. But since then, growth has taken off. Within a year, usage multiplied eightfold, climbing past four million miles by May 2025, the latest data available.

(This Data Insight was written by @redouad and Veronika Samborska.)

items.title: China's use of fertilizers peaked a decade ago— Since the 1960s, China’s population has more than doubled. Despite having more than twice as many mo...
items.description_html:
China's use of fertilizers peaked a decade ago—

Since the 1960s, China’s population has more than doubled. Despite having more than twice as many mouths to feed, the amount of food it produces per person has increased dramatically.

Better seeds, irrigation, pest management, and improved farming techniques have all helped increase the country’s agricultural productivity.

But the addition of nutrients through fertilizers has also made a huge difference. The chart shows the rapid uptake of fertilizers in China from the 1960s through the early 2000s.

While fertilizers can play a crucial role in feeding more people and using less land, they also have negative environmental impacts.

Excess nutrients run off into rivers and pollute coastlines, and fertilizers can emit nitrogen oxide, a powerful greenhouse gas. Using fertilizers more efficiently helps grow food while cutting pollution.

China has made important progress on this in the last decade. As you can see in the chart, its fertilizer use peaked in 2014 and has fallen since then. At the same time, the country’s agricultural production has continued to increase.

In 2015, China launched its “Zero-Growth Action Plan for Fertilizer”, and its government policies have played an essential role in this turnaround.

Subsidies previously made fertilizers very cheap in China, which encouraged farmers to overuse them. Cutting these subsidies while offering incentives for agricultural machinery, precision technologies, farmer education, and larger farms (which tend to use less fertilizer per hectare) has made China’s farming sector much more efficient.

(This Data Insight was written by @_HannahRitchie.)

items.title: A friend visited Japan for his honeymoon He was telling me Japan is so clean, that his white trousers, which he wore for 2 days while travelling on pu...
items.description_html:
A friend visited Japan for his honeymoon

He was telling me Japan is so clean, that his white trousers, which he wore for 2 days while travelling on public transport continuously, are still clean

Told me one more instance where he was asking a shopkeeper for some directions, and a customer came, but he made sure he helped him first before attending to the customer, despite telling him to attend to the customer

What a country! I am starting a SIP for a Japan trip now, calling it Systematic Japan Plan Fund!

Japan has been on my bucket list for some time now!

Vimta is a A 40-year discipline of compliance-first science, expanding into newer, higher-value verticals—while India’s testing needs deepen across pharma, food, environment, and electronics.

-Regulatory tightening in EMI/EMC and faster electronics exports.
-Higher offshore work as global sponsors diversify supply/testing footprints.

items.title: Re What did management hint on growth cadence? Management’s aspiration is to grow faster than industry (which itself is healthy), with biologics and ...
items.description_html:
Re What did management hint on growth cadence?

Management’s aspiration is to grow faster than industry (which itself is healthy), with biologics and electronics as newer engines atop the pharma/food core

Management have the aspiration to grow at 15-20% cagr which is almost double the Industry growth rate

items.title: Re Execution watchlist (next 4–6 quarters) -Biologics CR&D lab build milestones; first revenues in FY27. -Pharma testing capacity absorption post cap...
items.description_html:
Re Execution watchlist (next 4–6 quarters)

-Biologics CR&D lab build milestones; first revenues in FY27.
-Pharma testing capacity absorption post capex; throughput vs. utilization.
-EMI/EMC demand elasticity vs. new regulations/exports.
-Food lab network ramp, especially AP lab.
-Margin sustainability around 30%+ EBITDA amid competition/costs.

items.title: Re Moat mechanics Global audit track record, long-tenure public-sector/regulatory relationships (e.g., NFL), and early investments in lab automation/d...
items.description_html:
Re Moat mechanics

Global audit track record, long-tenure public-sector/regulatory relationships (e.g., NFL), and early investments in lab automation/digitization—which management flagged as a quality/throughput edge as the domestic lab landscape crowds.

items.title: Re What’s the theme and why do tailwinds favor Vimta? • Pharma/biopharma outsourcing rising with tighter US/EU regulations; India’s generic heft + ...
items.description_html:
Re What’s the theme and why do tailwinds favor Vimta?

• Pharma/biopharma outsourcing rising with tighter US/EU regulations; India’s generic heft + compliance-grade partners = advantage.
• Biosimilars and complex generics need deeper analytics.
• Biosecure/China-plus-one tilt nudges testing to India.
• Electronics/EV & defense push → more EMI/EMC.
• FSSAI enforcement + export compliance → food testing growth.

items.title: Re Capex & capacity runway FY25 capex ~₹79–80 cr; FY26 capex guided ~₹90 cr; ₹26 cr already spent in Q1—tilting towards equipment to unlock pharm...
items.description_html:
Re Capex & capacity runway

FY25 capex ~₹79–80 cr; FY26 capex guided ~₹90 cr; ₹26 cr already spent in Q1—tilting towards equipment to unlock pharma testing capacity and seed biologics CR&D.

items.title: Re The strategic reset 2024: diagnostic pathology (a lower-margin business) sold to Thyrocare; post-exit, consolidated EBITDA margins moved to ~35–37...
items.description_html:
Re The strategic reset

2024: diagnostic pathology (a lower-margin business) sold to Thyrocare; post-exit, consolidated EBITDA margins moved to ~35–37%, and management aimed to cross ₹500 cr revenue in the near term - freeing focus/capacity for higher-value testing.

items.title: Re Who are the customers? Partner to 90% of India’s top-20 pharma and 70% of top-10 food companies; plus PSUs & industrials; and the long-dated NFL o...
items.description_html:
Re Who are the customers?
Partner to 90% of India’s top-20 pharma and 70% of top-10 food companies; plus PSUs & industrials; and the long-dated NFL operations.

This isn’t transactional - it’s embedded.

items.title: Re Segment #4 – Environmental - EIA/EMP studies, ambient/stack emissions, water/soil testing, waste audits, and CEMS (continuous emissions monitoring...
items.description_html:
Re Segment #4 – Environmental

- EIA/EMP studies, ambient/stack emissions, water/soil testing, waste audits, and CEMS (continuous emissions monitoring) for ongoing compliance.

- Client base: Power, cement, metals/mining, O&G, infra, large manufacturing—segments under continuous scrutiny from SPCBs, CPCB, and lenders/insurers.

- Among the first gazette-notified EPA labs in India—regulatory recognition that raises the switching barrier for enterprise clients.

- Mix of long-cycle studies (EIA/clearances) + recurring monitoring contracts (CEMS, ambient networks), aiding volume predictability.

- Tailwinds: Stricter emission/water discharge norms and ESG disclosures drive breadth and frequency of tests; infra/manufacturing capex adds new sources.

items.title: Re Segment #3 – Electrical & Electronics (EMI/EMC) - Electromagnetic interference/compatibility certification for consumer electronics, automotive/EV...
items.description_html:
Re Segment #3 – Electrical & Electronics (EMI/EMC)

- Electromagnetic interference/compatibility certification for consumer electronics, automotive/EV components, medical devices, telecom, aerospace/defense—mandatory for market entry.

- NABL-accredited and BIS-recognized facility in Hyderabad; built to serve global-standard compliance programs.

- “Make in India” push across electronics/EVs, rising exports, and tightening product safety norms increase pre-shipment certification volumes.

- High fixed-asset intensity (chambers/equipment) with attractive marginal economics at scale; utilization ramps as customer mix broadens.

- Diversifies beyond life-sciences; opens doors to auto/medical-device cross-vertical programs with repeat compliance needs.

items.title: Re Segment #2 – Food & Agriculture (~15%) - What’s tested: Contaminants, residues, microbiology, nutritional labeling, packaging safety—aligned to ...
items.description_html:
Re Segment #2 – Food & Agriculture (~15%)

- What’s tested: Contaminants, residues, microbiology, nutritional labeling, packaging safety—aligned to FSSAI/BIS/APEDA/EU norms for domestic and export shipments.

- Seven labs across key consumption/port corridors (Hyderabad, Bengaluru, Ahmedabad, Mumbai, Noida, Nashik, Nellore) to shorten TAT for clients.

- Institutional anchor: Operates the National Food Laboratory (NFL) at JNPT under a 25-year FSSAI contract—an embedded, regulator-adjacent franchise that strengthens credibility and volumes.

- Demand drivers: Stricter domestic enforcement (FSSAI) + export compliance requirements; consumer shift toward clean-label/organic further expands test menus.

- Sticky, compliance-led volumes from large brands and processors; cross-sell into packaging, water, and environmental tests for food parks.

items.title: Re Segment #1 – Pharma & Biopharma (~60% of business) - BA/BE studies for generics, bioanalytical research, stability/impurity profiling, cGMP & non-...
items.description_html:
Re Segment #1 – Pharma & Biopharma (~60% of business)

- BA/BE studies for generics, bioanalytical research, stability/impurity profiling, cGMP & non-GMP analytical labs, and central lab services for clinical programs

- Who Buys their services - India’s top pharma/biopharma sponsors (incl. exporters) needing audit-ready data for US/EU/ROW submissions; Vimta’s multi-regulator accreditation lowers sponsor approval risk and time

- Revenue model & cadence: Project-based with repeat flows across the drug lifecycle (method dev → validation → stability → release), supporting utilization and margin visibility

- Revenue model & cadence: Project-based with repeat flows across the drug lifecycle (method dev → validation → stability → release), supporting utilization and margin visibility

- Why growth sustains: Tightening global compliance, biosimilars/complex generics, and outsourcing to India; post-diagnostics exit, mix is skewed to high-margin life-sciences work.

items.title: Re And this is how the journey of Vimta labs looks like till now - - Started from mineral and rice oil testing and moving towards Pharma and BioPharma...
items.description_html:
Re And this is how the journey of Vimta labs looks like till now -

- Started from mineral and rice oil testing and moving towards Pharma and BioPharma
- Exports contribute ~25–30%; facilities span 600,000+ sq ft. Among the first WHO pre-qualified labs in Asia; 90+ successful audits by global agencies—trust equity built over decades.

items.title: Re What exactly does Vimta do? Think of Vimta as a multi-discipline “quality nerve center” spanning Pharma & Biopharma testing, Food & Agriculture, ...
items.description_html:
Re What exactly does Vimta do?
Think of Vimta as a multi-discipline “quality nerve center” spanning Pharma & Biopharma testing, Food & Agriculture, Environmental, and Electrical/Electronics (EMI/EMC) compliance - with global-grade accreditations (US FDA, WHO, UK-MHRA, etc.)

items.title: Technology can change the world in ways that are unimaginable until they happen. Switching on an electric light would have been unimaginable for our m...
items.description_html:
Technology can change the world in ways that are unimaginable until they happen.

Switching on an electric light would have been unimaginable for our medieval ancestors. In their childhood, our grandparents would have struggled to imagine a world connected by smartphones and the Internet.

Similarly, it is hard for us to imagine the arrival of all those technologies that will fundamentally change the world we are used to.

We can remind ourselves that our own future might look very different from the world today by looking back at how rapidly technology has changed our world in the past.

One insight to take away from this long-term perspective is how unusual our time is.

Technological change was extremely slow in the past — the technologies that our ancestors got used to in their childhood were still central to their lives in their old age.

In stark contrast to those days, we live in a time of extraordinarily fast technological change. For recent generations, it was common for technologies that were unimaginable in their youth to become common later in life.

items.description_html:
Manish chokhani interview on my feed and I never miss it , do watch it folks ! @chokhani_manish

My notes -

1) May have resolution on Tariffs in next 3 months
2) AI theme is a norm , and move against dollar and into hard metals has been a strong trend
3) Ind vs US valuations ( Big 6 USA firms PAT 12 MT 500 billion USD$ , they trade 20-25 times valuation and some Indian firms demand much higher valuations in index companies - from FII POV )

4) Next 2-3 years Indian IT sector will have to rearrange datasets for AI , SBI pat overtakes reliance , new age as a% of participants in index will change
5) Financials that can leverage data and AI will lead to cost income ratios falling over the decade , at some point capital cycle does play out

6) IT must make the transition and pivot to be IP owners , if they don't change a slow derating may be on the cards , and same may be happening FMCG and Indian Generics also must invest far more into R&D , profit pools are leading to more Eli Lily models , they are outsourcing the risk to us as we are a microcap in global context
7) Avoid super expensive story themes , may prefer to be invested in already existing names
8) Food delivery aggregate market cap to earnings have to be watched closely
9) Non Strategic Disinvestment could be back on the cards in IDBI , NMDC very much beaten and neglected
10) On Domestic liquidity , a low base helps a lot more risk capital allocation , have to moderate SIP return expectations , >40% of 2 year fresh SIP market return has tapered down quoting sanjeev prasad from kotak
11) Look outside also as lots of good technology is growing in the world , change is in the air

Thanks for all your wisdom @chokhani_manish , great questions @AyeshaFaridi1

items.title: Six in ten workers in India are self-employed, making up the largest category of workers. India's labour statistics define the self-employed as person...
items.description_html:
Six in ten workers in India are self-employed, making up the largest category of workers.

India's labour statistics define the self-employed as persons who operate their own farm or non-farm enterprise, or are engaged independently in a profession or trade on their own, or with one or a few partners in household enterprises.

Unlike salaried and casual workers, the self-employed do not get paid a wage, but their earnings come from the profits of the enterprise.

The rural self-employed work largely in agriculture, where they are cultivators, or livestock and dairy producers, or engaged in allied activities like crop-cutting. The urban self-employed, meanwhile, are for the most part in services, where they run or work as salespersons in retail shops, or are wholesale traders, or work as self-employed drivers, among the key occupations.

In India, self-employment is higher in rural areas than in urban areas.

How does self-employment play out differently with respect to gender across India? Read @akwaghmare's piece to find out: https://www.dataforindia.com/self-employment/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

#Employment #SelfEmployment #Work #India #DataForIndia
